title: KV Cache
lesson:
When a language model writes an answer, it produces one token (a word or word-piece) at a time. To pick each new token, it runs attention — it looks back at every token already in the conversation and decides which ones matter. Here's the catch: without a trick, the model would re-read and re-process the entire conversation from scratch for every single new token. The KV cache is that trick. It is the single most important reason chatbots feel fast.

The expensive habit it fixes

Imagine you're writing a 1,000-word essay, but you have a strange rule: before adding each new word, you must re-read the whole essay so far from the very first word. Word 2 means re-reading 1 word. Word 500 means re-reading 499. Word 1,000 means re-reading 999. The re-reading piles up into a mountain of wasted effort. That is exactly what a model does without a cache: each new token forces it to recompute attention over all the previous tokens again.

The fix is obvious once you see it: remember your work. As you read each word the first time, jot a sticky note capturing what that word contributes. Next time, glance at the sticky notes instead of re-reading the page. The KV cache is that stack of sticky notes.

What "K" and "V" actually are

Attention turns every token into three vectors (lists of numbers): a query (what this token is looking for), a key (a label saying what this token offers), and a value (the actual content to mix in). When the model generates a brand-new token, only that new token needs a fresh query. But it must compare that query against the keys and values of every earlier token — and those never change once computed. So the model stores them. KV cache literally means "the saved Keys and Values" of all tokens seen so far. Each step it computes K and V for just the one new token, appends them to the cache, and reuses everything already there.

Think of a courtroom transcript. Every time someone speaks, the stenographer records it once and files it. To ask a new question, the lawyer doesn't make every previous witness repeat their whole testimony — they just flip to the filed transcript. The new question is the query; the filed testimony is the cached keys and values.

Two phases: prefill and decode

This splits generation into two stages. Prefill is processing your prompt: the model reads all your input tokens at once and fills the cache with their K and V. Decode is producing the answer: one token at a time, each reusing the cache and adding one new entry. Prefill is heavy but parallel; decode is light per step but happens many times. This is why the first token of a reply often takes longer than the rest ("time to first token") — prefill is doing the bulk reading up front.

from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B")
ids = tok("The capital of France is", return_tensors="pt").input_ids

# use_cache=True (the default) keeps and reuses the KV cache
out = model.generate(ids, max_new_tokens=20, use_cache=True)
print(tok.decode(out[0]))

Set use_cache=False and the same generation still works — but it gets dramatically slower on long text, because every step recomputes the past.

The cost: it eats memory

Speed isn't free. The cache lives in GPU memory (VRAM), and it grows with every token. Its size scales with sequence length times layers times attention heads times head size times two (one slab for K, one for V), per request. For a big model and a long context window, the cache can swell to several gigabytes — sometimes rivaling the model's own weights. A rough rule: doubling the context roughly doubles the cache. This is why long conversations and many simultaneous users strain a server, and it's the central problem that inference optimization tools tackle.

Serving engines fight back with clever cache management. vLLM uses PagedAttention, which stores the cache in small reusable blocks (like a memory page system) so it wastes far less space and can pack more users onto one GPU. Other tricks shrink each entry: grouped-query attention lets several query heads share one set of keys and values, and cache quantization stores K and V in fewer bits. All of these trade a sliver of quality or simplicity for a much smaller cache.

One more payoff: because a shared prompt prefix produces an identical cache, servers can compute it once and reuse it across many requests ("prefix caching") — a big win for chatbots that reuse the same long system prompt on every call.
mentalModel: The KV cache is a stack of sticky notes the model writes as it reads. Each new word it generates glances at the notes instead of re-reading the whole page — fast to use, but the stack grows taller (and hungrier for memory) with every token.
mistakes: Thinking the cache makes the model smarter or gives it memory. It changes nothing about the answer — it's purely a speed optimization. With or without the cache the output is identical; only the runtime differs. It is not long-term memory and it vanishes when the request ends.

Forgetting the cache uses memory that competes with everything else. The cache shares VRAM with the model weights and the activations. People load a model that 'fits' the GPU, then hit out-of-memory errors only once prompts get long — because the growing cache pushed them over the edge.

Assuming a longer context is free if you have the cache. Cache size grows roughly linearly with sequence length, so a 32k-token chat can use many times the memory of a 2k one. Long context windows cost real VRAM per user, not just compute.

Confusing the KV cache with Flash Attention. Flash Attention speeds up computing attention; the KV cache avoids recomputing the past. They solve different problems and are usually used together, not instead of each other.

Expecting prefix caching to kick in when the prefix isn't identical. Reuse only works if the leading tokens match exactly. Change one character early in a shared system prompt and the cached prefix is invalidated — so keep stable boilerplate first and the variable parts last.

Caching K and V for every new prompt in a batch but ignoring fragmentation. Naive cache allocation reserves a big contiguous slab per request and wastes most of it. Engines like vLLM exist precisely because that waste cripples how many users one GPU can serve.
exercise:
Using the Hugging Face snippet from the lesson (or any local model via Ollama/llama.cpp with timing), generate the same 100-token reply twice: once with use_cache=True and once with use_cache=False. Time both with Python's time.perf_counter(). Predict the gap before you run it, then confirm: the cached run should be far faster, and the gap should widen the longer the output gets. Write one sentence explaining why the no-cache version slows down as the text grows.

Stretch goal: Estimate the KV cache size for a model you use. Find its number of layers, key/value heads, and head dimension, then compute 2 (K and V) x layers x kv_heads x head_dim x bytes_per_number x sequence_length. Compare a 1k-token chat to a 32k-token chat and to the model's weight size — and decide whether your GPU could serve 10 such conversations at once.
solution: The no-cache version slows down because attention work at step n scales with all n previous tokens, so each new token costs more than the last; over a whole reply this becomes roughly quadratic, while the cached version stays roughly linear (one cheap new entry per step). For the stretch goal: plug your model's config numbers into the formula. A concrete example — a model with ~32 layers, 8 key/value heads, head dimension 128, stored at 2 bytes (FP16): per token that's 2 x 32 x 8 x 128 x 2 = 131,072 bytes, about 0.13 MB. At 1k tokens that's ~131 MB; at 32k tokens ~4.2 GB per conversation. Ten of those long chats would need ~42 GB of cache alone — likely more than a single consumer GPU has, which is exactly why batching and PagedAttention matter.
summary: The KV cache stores the keys and values of every token already processed so the model never re-reads the past — turning generation from a re-read-everything slog into one cheap step per new token. Work splits into a heavy prefill phase (reading the prompt) and many light decode steps. The price is GPU memory: the cache grows with context length and can rival the model's own size, which is why long contexts cost VRAM and why serving engines use tricks like PagedAttention, grouped-query attention, and prefix caching.

keyTerms.def: The stored Keys and Values of all tokens seen so far, kept in GPU memory so the model can reuse them instead of recomputing attention over the past at every step.
Two of the three vectors attention builds per token. Keys label what a token offers and values hold its content; both stay fixed once computed, so they are worth caching.
The third attention vector — what a token is looking for. The newest token always needs a fresh query, which is compared against all cached keys and values.
The first generation phase, where the model reads the whole input prompt at once and fills the cache with every prompt token's keys and values.
The second phase, where the model emits the answer one token at a time, reusing the cache and appending a single new entry each step.
How long before the first output token appears, dominated by prefill. Later tokens stream faster because they ride on the cache.
A vLLM technique that stores the KV cache in small reusable blocks (like memory pages) to cut waste and pack more users onto one GPU.
A model design where several query heads share one set of keys and values, shrinking the KV cache with little loss in quality.
Reusing the cached keys and values of a shared prompt prefix across requests, so identical boilerplate (like a system prompt) is processed once instead of every call.
